abstract: Minority populations remain significantly underrepresented in clinical research, a disparity that obscures the understanding of chronic diseases and limits the application of precision medicine in high-risk communities. This study explores the multifaceted barriers to recruitment in the United States, including historical mistrust, socioeconomic pressures, and linguistic obstacles. Drawing on decades of global experience in infectious disease research, the authors present a successful community-centric model implemented in the Texas Rio Grande Valley. Since 2004, this approach has utilized a team of locally recruited and trained staff—bilingual community members who share the culture of the participants—to enroll and retain a longitudinal cohort of over 5,000 Mexican Americans. By prioritizing trust, reducing participant burden through localized outreach, and providing feedback on research findings, this model has achieved high levels of engagement in complex multi-omic and clinical studies. The findings suggest that bridging the recruitment gap requires investigators to move beyond "parachute science" and invest in community infrastructure, transforming research into a collaborative partnership that benefits both scientific discovery and public health equity.
